title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
author: James Silberman
orientation: An account of pastor Derin Stidd's June 2019 motion at the Southern Baptist Convention urging the ERLC to support the immediate abolition of abortion, and a rebuke of former ERLC policy director Matt Hawkins (and ERLC communications specialist Joe Carter) for celebrating the motion's denial. The piece argues that SBC and ERLC leadership operate from a judicial supremacist, unbiblical posture that protects pro-life incrementalism and obstructs bills that would actually abolish abortion.
audiences: rank-and-file Southern Baptists evaluating their denominational leadership
abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
pastors weighing the church's prophetic duty against political pragmatism
state legislators and activists pushing equal protection bills
pro-lifers reconsidering judicial supremacy and Roe-era strategy

questions.question: What exactly did Pastor Derin Stidd's motion at SBC 2019 ask the ERLC to do?
questions.audience: rank-and-file Southern Baptists evaluating their denominational leadership
questions.answer: Stidd moved that the ERLC study how SBC churches, local associations, and state conventions could work with legislators and governors to promote the immediate abolition of human abortion, and that the SBC reaffirm its commitment to article 15 protecting the preborn. The motion explicitly rebuked SBC-affiliated state conventions in Texas and Oklahoma that had opposed bills which would have abolished abortion in those states.
questions.quote: I would like to make a motion that the ERLC of the Southern Baptist Convention do a study on how churches, local associations, and state conventions can work with all entities within their state including legislators, governors, and local associations to promote and admonish and encourage the immediate abolition of human abortion, and reaffirm our commitment to a Biblical view that all people are created in the image of God, and our commitment as Southern Baptists to article 15 which speaks to the protection of the preborn.

questions.question: On what grounds did the SBC Executive Committee deny Stidd's motion, and was that reasoning unavoidable?
questions.audience: rank-and-file Southern Baptists evaluating their denominational leadership
questions.answer: President J.D. Grear announced the next morning that the motion was denied because the convention can only request, not direct, an entity. Silberman argues this was a discretionary technicality the committee could easily have fixed by amending the wording to 'requesting,' allowing a real debate on abolition bills instead of killing the motion outright.
questions.quote: The handling of technicalities like this one are largely up to the discretion of the committee. They could have amended the motion to address Stidd's concerns and facilitate a discussion on bills which abolish abortion.

questions.question: How did Matt Hawkins characterize the abolitionist position, and what did he celebrate?
questions.audience: abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Hawkins, the former ERLC policy director, took to Twitter to deride immediatism as 'pointless perfectionism' and to praise Southern Baptist messengers for crushing it. He framed the rejection of Stidd's motion as the SBC astutely defeating an extreme, fantastical position rather than as the convention sidestepping a serious challenge to pro-life lobbying.
questions.quote: At #SBC19 an assertion of extreme immediacism (I call it pointless perfectionism) on #prolife lawmaking crept into a floor debate. Southern Baptist messengers astutely squashed the fantasy like the bug it is.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say Hawkins's 'pointless perfectionism' label is theologically bankrupt?
questions.audience: pastors weighing the church's prophetic duty against political pragmatism
questions.answer: Because Hawkins's premise treats the Supreme Court as an authority that cannot be defied, which is neither biblical nor constitutional. Scripture's response to cultures practicing child sacrifice is never to negotiate a compromise; prophetic speech demands immediate repentance, so calling immediatism 'perfectionist' reveals an atheological starting point.
questions.quote: Biblical examples of speaking Godly truth to a culture practicing child sacrifice never include starting from an assumption that a compromise on child sacrifice should be struck (or some other evil). Prophetic exhortations are always calls to immediate repentance.

questions.question: How does the article rebut the claim that immediatists 'have nothing to show for their efforts'?
questions.audience: abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Silberman responds that anti-abortion immediatism is less than a decade old and has already accomplished what the pro-life movement could not in 46 years: getting actual abolition bills introduced. The reason those bills haven't passed in red states isn't immediatist failure but active opposition from SBC-affiliated conventions and pro-life incrementalists who kill the bills in committee.
questions.quote: Further, Hawkins and Carter also are speaking from complete ignorance regarding the history of anti-abortion immediatism, which has existed for less than a decade. In that time, we've done what the pro-life movement hasn't been able to do in 46 years: introduce bills which abolish abortion. Again, these bills would be passing were it not for the pro-life incrementalists who are killing them in committee.

questions.question: What evidence does the article give that SBC and ERLC leaders don't speak for ordinary Southern Baptists on abortion?
questions.audience: rank-and-file Southern Baptists evaluating their denominational leadership
questions.answer: Stidd's motion drew a 'significant cheer' from the floor, attendees personally thanked him afterward, and in Oklahoma Southern Baptists organized an entire rally and an hour-long public forum and authored a petition letter rebuking the BGCO for opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. Silberman frames this as proof of a widening gap between the cooperative-program-funded leadership and the rank-and-file.
questions.quote: This was made abundantly clear in Oklahoma as an entire rally was led by Southern Baptists to rebuke the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma (BGCO) which opposed the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. On top of that, a gathering a Southern Baptist pastors and elders led an hour-long public forum on the matter and authored a petition letter addressed to the BGCO.

questions.question: What does Stidd say the ERLC's prophetic standard should actually be?
questions.audience: pastors weighing the church's prophetic duty against political pragmatism
questions.answer: Stidd argues that the ERLC, as the prophetic voice of the SBC to the culture, must measure right and wrong on abortion by the unchanging word of God rather than by what the Supreme Court will tolerate. The article extends this point to call out Graham, Grear, Hawkins, and Carter for behaving as humanistic political operatives rather than prophets.
questions.quote: It is important for the ERLC, which is supposed to be the prophetic voice of the SBC to the culture, that our standard of right and wrong as it relates to abortion is not built upon what the supreme court might permit, but rather is built upon the unchanging word of God.

questions.question: What constitutional argument does the article offer for ending the deference to SCOTUS on abortion?
questions.audience: state legislators and activists pushing equal protection bills
questions.answer: Silberman insists the courts must be put in their rightful place beneath the Constitution and beneath God by proclaiming that human life begins at conception and that the 14th Amendment was written precisely to forbid the separation of 'person' from 'human being.' He couples this with the prophetic warning, 'Woe to those make iniquitous decrees.'
questions.quote: It's time to stop begging the tyrant for scraps and put them in their rightful place beneath the Constitution and ultimately beneath God by proclaiming the truth that human life begins at conception, that the separation of "person" from "human being" is what the 14th amendment was written to prohibit, and to warn them, "Woe to those make iniquitous decrees."

questions.question: What does the author claim happened when he previously published criticism of Texas and Oklahoma Baptist leaders?
questions.audience: pro-lifers reconsidering judicial supremacy and Roe-era strategy
questions.answer: Silberman says he wrote a piece for a prominent conservative publication exposing how Texas and Oklahoma Baptist leaders helped prevent abortion's abolition; high-level SBC figures responded by bombarding his editors with emails and calls for nearly a month until the article was pulled and an apology posted, even though nothing in it was inaccurate. He cites this as evidence of the SBC's willingness to wield political and cultural power against critics.
questions.quote: High-level SBC people responded by bombarding my editors with emails and phone calls for almost a month and got the article taken down and an apology posted despite nothing in the article being inaccurate. This is what we're up against.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Direct follow-up showing Oklahoma Baptists pushing back against the BGCO leadership the SBC motion rebuked.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Concrete example of the SBC-affiliated Baptist leadership opposition to abolition bills that Stidd's motion called out.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the same critique of judicial supremacism that Silberman levels at Hawkins and the ERLC.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Facade
related.reason: Documents the Texas pro-life and Baptist machinery that worked against the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act referenced in this article.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Companion piece on the Texas legislative defeat that fueled abolitionists' frustration with pro-life leadership at SBC 2019.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Foundational explainer for the immediatism that Hawkins derides as 'pointless perfectionism' in this article.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the abolitionist-vs-pro-life distinction that drives the conflict between Stidd's motion and ERLC leadership.
